---
title: "Untitled"
author: "Shah"
date: "2026-06-18"
output:
  html_document: default
  word_document: default
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

# =============================================================
# PLFS 2019-20: Returns to Education — Data Cleaning Pipeline
# Files used: PERFV_2019-20.csv, PERRV_2019-20.csv
# =============================================================

# ---- 1. Load packages ----
library(data.table)   # fast read/write for large CSVs
library(tidyverse)    # cleaning, wrangling

setwd("D:/Projects (ongoing)/Returns to Education")

# ---- 2. Read raw files (fast, handles 100MB+ files well) ----
perfv <- fread("D:/Projects (ongoing)/Returns to Education/CSV_PLFS_19_20/PERFV_2019-20.csv", na.strings = c("", "NA"))
perrv <- fread("D:/Projects (ongoing)/Returns to Education/CSV_PLFS_19_20/PERRV_2019-20.csv", na.strings = c("", "NA"))

dim(perfv)   # ~418,297 x 129
dim(perrv)   # ~523,163 x 104

# ---- 3. Select and rename only the variables we need ----
# (column names differ slightly between FV and RV — map both to common names)

perfv_sel <- perfv %>%
  select(
    state       = state_per_fv,
    sector      = b1q3_per_fv,
    sex         = b4q5_per_fv,
    age         = b4q6_per_fv,
    edu_general = b4q8_per_fv,
    edu_years   = b4q10_per_fv,
    cws_status  = b6q5_per_fv,
    industry    = b6q6_per_fv,
    wage        = b6q9_per_fv,
    weight      = MULT_per_fv
  ) %>%
  mutate(visit_type = "FV")

perrv_sel <- perrv %>%
  select(
    state       = state_per_rv,
    sector      = b1q3_per_rv,
    sex         = b4q5_per_rv,
    age         = b4q6_per_rv,
    edu_general = b4q8_per_rv,
    edu_years   = b4q10_per_rv,
    cws_status  = b6q5_per_rv,
    industry    = b6q6_per_rv,
    wage        = b6q9_per_rv,
    weight      = MULT_per_rv
  ) %>%
  mutate(visit_type = "RV")

# ---- 4. Stack (combine) both visits into one dataset ----
plfs_combined <- bind_rows(perfv_sel, perrv_sel)

cat("Combined rows before any filtering:", nrow(plfs_combined), "\n")

# ---- 5. Type conversion ----
# Some columns may load as character due to blanks; force numeric
plfs_combined <- plfs_combined %>%
  mutate(
    state       = as.numeric(state),
    sector      = as.numeric(sector),
    sex         = as.numeric(sex),
    age         = as.numeric(age),
    edu_general = as.numeric(edu_general),
    edu_years   = as.numeric(edu_years),
    cws_status  = as.numeric(cws_status),
    wage        = as.numeric(wage),
    weight      = as.numeric(weight)
  )

# ---- 6. Filter to wage/salaried employees only ----
# CWS code 31 = Regular salaried/wage employee (the only group with
# valid monthly earnings data in b6q9, per official PLFS manual)
plfs_wage <- plfs_combined %>%
  filter(cws_status == 31)

cat("Rows after CWS==31 filter:", nrow(plfs_wage), "\n")

# ---- 7. Apply standard sample restrictions ----
plfs_clean <- plfs_wage %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(wage), wage > 0,            # valid positive earnings
    !is.na(edu_years),                  # valid education info
    !is.na(age),
    age >= 15, age <= 65,                # working-age population
    sex %in% c(1, 2)                     # drop third-gender (data too sparse for separate analysis)
  )

cat("Rows after sample restrictions:", nrow(plfs_clean), "\n")

# ---- 8. Construct model variables ----
plfs_final <- plfs_clean %>%
  mutate(
    # Dependent variable
    log_wage = log(wage),

    # Mincer potential experience
    experience    = age - edu_years - 6,
    experience_sq = experience^2,

    # Dummies
    male  = ifelse(sex == 1, 1, 0),
    urban = ifelse(sector == 2, 1, 0),

    # Readable education category (for descriptive tables/checks)
    edu_level_label = case_when(
      edu_general == 1  ~ "Not literate",
      edu_general %in% c(2,3,4) ~ "Literate, no formal schooling",
      edu_general == 5  ~ "Below primary",
      edu_general == 6  ~ "Primary",
      edu_general == 7  ~ "Middle",
      edu_general == 8  ~ "Secondary",
      edu_general == 10 ~ "Higher secondary",
      edu_general == 11 ~ "Diploma/certificate",
      edu_general == 12 ~ "Graduate",
      edu_general == 13 ~ "Postgraduate+",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  # Drop implausible experience (negative experience = data entry issue
  # or someone still very young relative to reported education years)
  filter(experience >= 0, experience <= 50)

cat("Final analysis sample:", nrow(plfs_final), "\n")

# ---- 9. Quick sanity checks ----
summary(plfs_final$wage)
summary(plfs_final$edu_years)
summary(plfs_final$experience)
table(plfs_final$male)
table(plfs_final$urban)
table(plfs_final$edu_level_label)

# Check for any remaining missing values in key model variables
plfs_final %>%
  select(log_wage, edu_years, experience, experience_sq, male, urban, state) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~sum(is.na(.))))

# ---- 10. Save cleaned dataset for analysis stage ----
saveRDS(plfs_final, "plfs_2019_20_clean.rds")
fwrite(plfs_final, "plfs_2019_20_clean.csv")

cat("\n=== Cleaning complete ===\n")
cat("Final sample size:", nrow(plfs_final), "observations\n")
cat("Saved to: plfs_2019_20_clean.rds / .csv\n")

# ---- Additional cleaning: trim implausible wage values ----
# Drop near-zero wages (likely data/coding artifacts, not real earnings)
plfs_trimmed <- plfs_final %>%
  filter(wage >= 500)   # ₹500/month is a generous lower bound; anything below
                         # is not a credible regular-salary observation

# Winsorize (cap) extreme top earners at 99th percentile to reduce leverage
# of a small number of outliers, while keeping them in the sample
wage_99 <- quantile(plfs_trimmed$wage, 0.99)
plfs_trimmed <- plfs_trimmed %>%
  mutate(
    wage_capped = pmin(wage, wage_99),
    log_wage_capped = log(wage_capped)
  )

cat("Rows before trim:", nrow(plfs_final), "\n")
cat("Rows after trim:", nrow(plfs_trimmed), "\n")
cat("99th percentile wage cap:", wage_99, "\n")

saveRDS(plfs_trimmed, "plfs_2019_20_clean_trimmed.rds")
fwrite(plfs_trimmed, "plfs_2019_20_clean_trimmed.csv")

# =============================================================
# PLFS 2019-20: Mincer Earnings Function — Regression Analysis
# =============================================================

library(tidyverse)
library(stargazer)
library(sandwich)
library(lmtest)

# ---- Load cleaned, trimmed data ----
plfs <- readRDS("plfs_2019_20_clean_trimmed.rds")

# Use log_wage_capped as primary dependent variable (winsorized)
# log_wage (uncapped) reserved for robustness check later

# ---- Model 1: Basic Mincer (education only) ----
model1 <- lm(log_wage_capped ~ edu_years, data = plfs)

# ---- Model 2: Classic Mincer (+ experience) ----
model2 <- lm(log_wage_capped ~ edu_years + experience + experience_sq,
             data = plfs)

# ---- Model 3: Extended (+ gender, sector) ----
model3 <- lm(log_wage_capped ~ edu_years + experience + experience_sq +
               male + urban,
             data = plfs)

# ---- Model 4: Full model with state fixed effects ----
model4 <- lm(log_wage_capped ~ edu_years + experience + experience_sq +
               male + urban + factor(state),
             data = plfs)

# ---- Comparison table ----
stargazer(model1, model2, model3, model4,
          type = "html",
          title = "Mincer Earnings Function — PLFS 2019-20",
          dep.var.labels = "Log Monthly Wage (winsorized at 99th pctile)",
          covariate.labels = c("Years of Education", "Experience",
                               "Experience²", "Male", "Urban"),
          omit = "factor(state)",
          add.lines = list(c("State Fixed Effects", "No","No","No","Yes")),
          omit.stat = c("f","ser"),
          digits = 4,
          out="Comparison Table.html",
          align=TRUE)

# ---- Key result: % return per year of education ----
cat("\n--- RETURNS TO EDUCATION ---\n")
cat("Model 1 (basic):   ", round((exp(coef(model1)["edu_years"]) - 1) * 100, 2), "%\n")
cat("Model 2 (+exp):    ", round((exp(coef(model2)["edu_years"]) - 1) * 100, 2), "%\n")
cat("Model 3 (+gender/sector):", round((exp(coef(model3)["edu_years"]) - 1) * 100, 2), "%\n")
cat("Model 4 (+state FE):", round((exp(coef(model4)["edu_years"]) - 1) * 100, 2), "%\n")

# ---- Robustness check: same model on uncapped log_wage ----
model4_uncapped <- lm(log_wage ~ edu_years + experience + experience_sq +
                        male + urban + factor(state), data = plfs)
cat("\nModel 4 return using UNCAPPED wage (robustness check):",
    round((exp(coef(model4_uncapped)["edu_years"]) - 1) * 100, 2), "%\n")

# =============================================================
# Heteroskedasticity check + Robust Standard Errors
# =============================================================

# Breusch-Pagan test
bptest(model4)
# If p < 0.05 (very likely with survey wage data), use robust SEs below

# Robust SE (HC3) — recommended for cross-sectional wage data
robust_se_model4 <- coeftest(model4, vcov = vcovHC(model4, type = "HC3"))
print(robust_se_model4)

# Side-by-side: default vs robust SE for key coefficient
cat("\n--- Education coefficient: OLS default SE vs Robust SE ---\n")
cat("Default SE: ", summary(model4)$coefficients["edu_years", "Std. Error"], "\n")
cat("Robust SE:  ", robust_se_model4["edu_years", "Std. Error"], "\n")


# =============================================================
# PLFS 2019-20: Subgroup Analysis — Gender & Sector
# =============================================================

library(tidyverse)
library(stargazer)
library(sandwich)
library(lmtest)

plfs <- readRDS("plfs_2019_20_clean_trimmed.rds")

# ---- Subgroup samples ----
plfs_male   <- filter(plfs, male == 1)
plfs_female <- filter(plfs, male == 0)
plfs_rural  <- filter(plfs, urban == 0)
plfs_urban  <- filter(plfs, urban == 1)

cat("Sample sizes:\n")
cat("Male:  ", nrow(plfs_male), "\n")
cat("Female:", nrow(plfs_female), "\n")
cat("Rural: ", nrow(plfs_rural), "\n")
cat("Urban: ", nrow(plfs_urban), "\n")

# ---- Gender-split models (mirrors Model 4: full controls + state FE) ----
model_male <- lm(log_wage_capped ~ edu_years + experience + experience_sq +
                    urban + factor(state),
                  data = plfs_male)

model_female <- lm(log_wage_capped ~ edu_years + experience + experience_sq +
                      urban + factor(state),
                    data = plfs_female)

# ---- Sector-split models ----
model_rural <- lm(log_wage_capped ~ edu_years + experience + experience_sq +
                     male + factor(state),
                   data = plfs_rural)

model_urban <- lm(log_wage_capped ~ edu_years + experience + experience_sq +
                     male + factor(state),
                   data = plfs_urban)

# ---- Comparison table (state FE omitted from display for readability) ----
stargazer(model_male, model_female, model_rural, model_urban,
          type = "html",
          title = "Returns to Education by Gender and Sector",
          column.labels = c("Male", "Female", "Rural", "Urban"),
          dep.var.labels = "Log Monthly Wage (winsorized)",
          covariate.labels = c("Years of Education", "Experience",
                               "Experience²", "Urban", "Male"),
          omit = "factor(state)",
          add.lines = list(c("State Fixed Effects", "Yes","Yes","Yes","Yes")),
          omit.stat = c("f","ser"),
          digits = 4,
          out="Subgroup Comparison Table.html",
          align=TRUE)

# ---- Extract and compare the headline number across subgroups ----
get_return <- function(model, var = "edu_years") {
  round((exp(coef(model)[var]) - 1) * 100, 2)
}

cat("\n--- RETURNS TO EDUCATION BY SUBGROUP ---\n")
cat("Male:   ", get_return(model_male), "%\n")
cat("Female: ", get_return(model_female), "%\n")
cat("Rural:  ", get_return(model_rural), "%\n")
cat("Urban:  ", get_return(model_urban), "%\n")
cat("Pooled (Model 4, for reference): 10.25 %\n")

# =============================================================
# Formal test: is the male-female difference in returns
# statistically significant? (Chow-style test via interaction)
# =============================================================

model_interact <- lm(log_wage_capped ~ edu_years + experience + experience_sq +
                        urban + male + edu_years:male + factor(state),
                      data = plfs)

summary(model_interact)$coefficients["edu_years:male", ]

cat("\nInterpretation: if edu_years:male is significant, the gender\n",
    "gap in RETURNS (not just levels) is statistically real, not\n",
    "just sampling noise between the two separate regressions.\n")

# Same logic for rural/urban
model_interact_sector <- lm(log_wage_capped ~ edu_years + experience + experience_sq +
                               male + urban + edu_years:urban + factor(state),
                             data = plfs)

summary(model_interact_sector)$coefficients["edu_years:urban", ]

# ---- Robust SEs for the interaction models (heteroskedasticity persists) ----
coeftest(model_interact, vcov = vcovHC(model_interact, type = "HC3"))["edu_years:male", ]
coeftest(model_interact_sector, vcov = vcovHC(model_interact_sector, type = "HC3"))["edu_years:urban", ]




library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# ---- Chart 1: Progressive models ----
model_returns <- data.frame(
  model = factor(c("Model 1\n(Basic)", "Model 2\n(+Experience)",
                    "Model 3\n(+Gender/Sector)", "Model 4\n(+State FE)"),
                  levels = c("Model 1\n(Basic)", "Model 2\n(+Experience)",
                             "Model 3\n(+Gender/Sector)", "Model 4\n(+State FE)")),
  return_pct = c(8.00, 10.62, 10.43, 10.25),
  highlight = c("No","No","No","Yes")
)

ggplot(model_returns, aes(x = model, y = return_pct, fill = highlight)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.55) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(return_pct, "%")), vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold", size = 4.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("No" = "grey60", "Yes" = "#1f3a5f"), guide = "none") +
  labs(title = "Returns to Education Stabilize as Controls Are Added",
       y = "Return to one additional year of education (%)", x = NULL) +
  ylim(0, 14) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
ggsave("chart1_Progressive_Models.png", width = 9, height = 5.8, dpi = 200)

# ---- Chart 2: Gender & sector subgroup bars ----
subgroup_df <- data.frame(
  category = factor(c("Male","Female","Rural","Urban"), levels = c("Male","Female","Rural","Urban")),
  return_pct = c(10.02, 12.31, 8.92, 10.99),
  facet = c("Gender","Gender","Sector","Sector")
)

ggplot(subgroup_df, aes(x = category, y = return_pct, fill = category)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 10.25, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(return_pct, "%")), vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  facet_wrap(~facet, scales = "free_x") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Male"="#1f3a5f","Female"="#e76f51","Rural"="#2a9d8f","Urban"="#e9c46a")) +
  labs(title = "Returns to Education Vary by Gender and Sector",
       y = "Return to education (%)", x = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
  
ggsave("chart2_Gender_and_sector_subgroups.png", width = 9, height = 5.8, dpi = 200)

# ---- Chart 3: Wage-experience profile ----
exp_seq <- seq(0, 50, length.out = 200)
beta_exp  <- coef(model4)["experience"]
beta_exp2 <- coef(model4)["experience_sq"]
profile_df <- data.frame(
  experience = exp_seq,
  wage_growth_pct = (exp(beta_exp * exp_seq + beta_exp2 * exp_seq^2) - 1) * 100
)

ggplot(profile_df, aes(x = experience, y = wage_growth_pct)) +
  geom_area(fill = "#1f3a5f", alpha = 0.08) +
  geom_line(color = "#1f3a5f", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Wages Rise with Experience, at a Decreasing Rate",
       x = "Years of Potential Experience", y = "Wage Growth Relative to Entry (%)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
ggsave("chart3_wage_experience_profile.png", width = 9, height = 5.8, dpi = 200)  

# ---- Chart 4: Wage premiums (male, urban) ----
premium_df <- data.frame(
  group = factor(c("Urban wage\npremium","Male wage\npremium"),
                  levels = c("Urban wage\npremium","Male wage\npremium")),
  premium_pct = c((exp(coef(model4)["urban"]) - 1) * 100,
                   (exp(coef(model4)["male"]) - 1) * 100)
)

ggplot(premium_df, aes(x = group, y = premium_pct, fill = group)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0("+", round(premium_pct,1), "%")), hjust = -0.1, fontface = "bold") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Urban wage\npremium" = "#2a9d8f", "Male wage\npremium" = "#1f3a5f")) +
  labs(title = "Unexplained Wage Premiums (Model 4)",
       y = "Wage premium, holding education/experience/state constant (%)", x = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
  
ggsave("chart4_wage_premiums.png", width = 9, height = 5.8, dpi = 200)

# =============================================================
# PLFS 2019-20: Quantile Regression — Returns to Education Across Wage Distribution
# =============================================================

library(quantreg)
library(purrr)
library(dplyr)

quantiles <- c(0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.90)

# Fit one quantile at a time, store results in a list
qr_fits <- map(quantiles, function(q) {
  rq(log_wage_capped ~ edu_years + experience + experience_sq + male + urban,
     tau = q, method = "pfn", data = plfs)
})

names(qr_fits) <- paste0("Q", quantiles*100)

# Get asymptotic (nid) SEs for each fit separately
qr_summaries <- map(qr_fits, ~ summary(.x, se = "nid"))

# Extract edu_years coefficient + SE from each
edu_results <- map_df(seq_along(quantiles), function(i) {
  coefs <- qr_summaries[[i]]$coefficients
  data.frame(
    quantile  = quantiles[i],
    estimate  = coefs["edu_years", "Value"],
    std_error = coefs["edu_years", "Std. Error"]
  )
}) %>%
  mutate(
    return_pct = (exp(estimate) - 1) * 100,
    ci_lower   = (exp(estimate - 1.96 * std_error) - 1) * 100,
    ci_upper   = (exp(estimate + 1.96 * std_error) - 1) * 100
  )

print(edu_results)

# ---- Chart: Returns to Education Across Wage Distribution ----
ggplot(edu_results, aes(x = quantile, y = return_pct)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = ci_lower, ymax = ci_upper), fill = "#1f3a5f", alpha = 0.13) +
  geom_line(color = "#1f3a5f", linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_point(color = "#1f3a5f", size = 3.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 10.25, linetype = "dashed", color = "#e76f51", linewidth = 1.2) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.88, y = 10.65, label = "OLS mean: 10.25%",
           color = "#e76f51", fontface = "bold", size = 3.8) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.90),
                     labels = c("Q10\n(Low earners)","Q25","Q50\n(Median)","Q75","Q90\n(High earners)")) +
  labs(title = "Returns to Education Across the Wage Distribution",
       x = "Wage Quantile", y = "Return to one year of education (%)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

ggsave("chart5_quantile_regression.png", width = 9, height = 5.8, dpi = 200)
